standard_description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide. (Students need not use formal terms for these properties.) ^( )Examples: If 6 × 4 = 24 is known, then 4 × 6 = 24 is also known. (Commutative property of multiplication.) 3 × 5 × 2 can be found by 3 × 5 = 15, then 15 × 2 = 30, or by 5 × 2 = 10, then 3 × 10 = 30. (Associative property of multiplication.) Knowing that 8 × 5 = 40 and 8 × 2 = 16, one can find 8 × 7 as 8 × (5 + 2) = (8 × 5) + (8 × 2) = 40 + 16 = 56. (Distributive property.)
subject: Math

items.question_content:
Let's use the associative property to help us solve $28\times2$.

**Complete the equations by breaking $28$ into two smaller factors.**

| |
-:|:-
$\maroonE{28} \times \blueE2 = $| [[☃ dropdown 1]]${} \times \blueE2$|
$\maroonE{28} \times \blueE2 = $ | $7 \times{}$ [[☃ numeric-input 5]] |
$\maroonE{28} \times \blueE2 = $|  [[☃ numeric-input 4]]|
hints:
To find the product of $\maroonE{28}\times\blueE{2}$, we can break $\maroonE{28}$ into smaller factors.

We will keep the expressions on both sides of the equal sign equivalent.
The expression $7\times4$ equals $28$.

So, $\maroonE{28}$ is the same as $\maroonE{7}$ groups of $\maroonE{4}$.

$\maroonE{28} \times \blueE{2} ={ \maroonE{7}}\times \maroonE{4} \times \blueE{2}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
\maroonE{28} \times \blueE{2} &= \maroonE{7}\times \maroonE{4} \times \blueE{2}\\\\
&= 7 \times (4 \times 2)\\\\
&= 7 \times 8 \\\\
&= 56
\end{align}$

These are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\maroonE{28} \times \blueE{2} &=\maroonE{7}\times \maroonE{4} \times \blueE{2}\\\\
\maroonE{28} \times \blueE{2} &={7}\times \purpleE8\\\\
\maroonE{28} \times \blueE{2} &= 56
\end{align}$

$\maroonE{14} \times \blueE{3} =$|  [[☃ numeric-input 2]]|
hints:
To find the product of $\maroonE{14}\times\blueE{3}$, we can break $\maroonE{14}$ into smaller factors.

We will keep the expressions on both sides of the equal sign equivalent.
The expression $7\times 2$ equals $14$.

$\maroonE{14}$ is the same as $\maroonE{7}$ groups of $\maroonE{2}$.

$\maroonE{14} \times \blueE{3} =\maroonE{7} \times {\maroonE{2}} \times \blueE{3}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
\maroonE{14} \times \blueE{3} &={ \maroonE{7}}\times \maroonE{2} \times \blueE{3}\\\\
&= 7 \times (2 \times 3)\\\\
&= 7 \times 6 \\\\
&= 42
\end{align}$

These are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\maroonE{14} \times \blueE{3} &= \maroonE{7}\times \maroonE{2} \times \blueE{3}\\\\
\maroonE{14} \times \blueE{3} &= {7}\times 6\\\\
\maroonE{14} \times \blueE{3} &= 42
\end{align}$

khan_exercises.title: Represent area with the distributive property
khan_exercises.description: Use area models to represent the distributive property in finding area of rectangles.
topic_path: Topic C: Arithmetic properties using area models
Represent area with the distributive property

items.question_content:
One expression for the area of a $3$ unit by $8$ unit rectangle is $3\times 8$. 

We can also break the figure into two parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What are $2$ more expressions that represent the area of the entire rectangle?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The rectangle has an area of $3\times 8$, or  $3$ eights.

Let's use the figure to break the $3$ eights into two parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

The area of the top rectangle is $\blueE{4\text{ threes}}$ or $\blueE{12}$.

The area of the bottom rectangle is 
$\purpleE{4\text{ threes}}$ or $\purpleE{12}$.
We get the total area of the rectangle by adding the areas of the two parts.

The following expressions represent the area of the entire figure:

- $\blueE{4 \text{ threes}} +\purpleE{4\text{ threes}}$
- $\blueE{12} +\purpleE{12}$

The first 4 rows are shaded blue and a curved bracket across these rows is labeled 4. The last 4 rows are shaded purple and a curved bracket across these rows is labeled 4.

items.question_content:
One expression for the area of a $8$ unit by $3$ unit rectangle is $8\times 3$.

We can also break the figure into two parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What are $2$ more expressions that represent the area of the entire rectangle?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The rectangle has an area of $8\times3$, or $8$ threes.

Let's use the figure to break the $8$ threes into two parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

The area of the left rectangle is $\purpleD{3\times4}$ or $\purpleD{12}$.

The area of the right rectangle is $\goldE{3\times4}$ or $\goldE{12}$.
We get the total area of the rectangle by adding the areas of the two parts.

The following expressions represent the area of the entire figure:

- $\purpleD{12} + \goldE{12}$
- $(\purpleD{3\times4}) + (\goldE{3\times4})$

Kaley solved $\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times\maroonE{2}$ using the equations below.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &=(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{2}\\\\ 
&=50 \times\maroonE{2}\\\\\  
&=100
\end{align}$

**Use the equations below to solve $\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2}$ in a different way.**

||
-:|:-
$\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2}$ | $=\redE{10}\times($ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]${}\times\maroonE{2})$  |
|$= \redE{10}\times {}$  [[☃ numeric-input 3]]|
|$= 100$|
hints: The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Kaley solved the equation by multiplying $(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5})$ first, and then multiplying by $\maroonE{2}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &=(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{2}\\\\
&=\blueE{50}\times\maroonE{2}\\\\
&=100
\end{align}$

In the new equation below, we want to multiply $(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})$ first, and then multiply by $\redE{10}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &= \redE{10}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})\\\\
&=\redE{10}\times\goldE{10}\\\\ 
&=100
\end{align}$

Here are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &= \redE{10}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})\\\\
&=\redE{10}\times\goldE{10}\\\\ 
&=100
\end{align}$

khan_exercises.title: Multiply monomials by polynomials
khan_exercises.description: Use the distributive property to express the product of a monomial and a polynomial as a single polynomial.

items.question_content:
**Expand.**  
*Your answer should be a polynomial in standard form.*

$ 3x(x^2-5x+6)=$ [[☃ expression 2]]
hints:
We expand the parentheses using the **distributive property**:

$\blueD A(B+C+D)=\blueD A\cdot B+\blueD A\cdot C+\blueD A\cdot D$

We can also think about the problem using an area model:

[[☃ image 1]]

Here's how the solution goes, algebraically:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\goldE{3x}(x^2-5x+6)
\\\\
&=\goldE{3x}(x^2)+\goldE{3x}(-5x)+\goldE{3x}(6)
\\\\
&=3x^3-15x^2+18x
\end{align}$

Here's how the solution looks in terms of the area model:

[[☃ image 1]]

In conclusion,

$ 3x(x^2-5x+6)=3x^3-15x^2+18x$
